Create a Blazor Web App for building inspectors on construction sites.

Requirements:
- Inspectors fill out safety checklists and add notes at each site visit
- Photos are attached to inspection reports
- Construction sites frequently have poor or no cellular connectivity — the app must continue working offline
- When connectivity returns, data syncs back to the server
- Inspectors use phones and tablets, not desktops
- Low-latency UI is critical — inspectors tap through checklist items quickly
- No authentication needed in the initial version

# Create a Blazor Web App

## Before You Start — Gather Requirements

If the user's request doesn't make the following clear, ask before scaffolding:

1. **What does the app do?** List the main screens/features (e.g., "product catalog with search and shopping cart").
2. **What kind of interactivity is needed?** Displaying data and forms? Real-time updates? Offline support? Rich drag-and-drop UI?
3. **Deployment environment?** Internet-facing? Intranet? Mobile users on slow connections?
4. **Authentication needed?** Anonymous? Individual accounts? Organizational (Azure AD)?

## Pick the Right Interactivity Level

Blazor render modes are a progression scale. Start at the simplest level that satisfies the requirements and only move up when there's a concrete reason.

```
Static SSR ──→ SSR + Enhanced Nav ──→ Interactive Server ──→ Interactive WebAssembly
 simplest                                                           most complex
```

### Decision Rules

| If the app needs... | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Display data, simple forms, links between pages | **Static SSR** (`-int None`) | No JS runtime, no circuit, no WebAssembly download. Forms work via HTML POST. Enhanced navigation makes it feel snappy. |
| Everything above + a few components with client-side behavior (live search, real-time updates, complex form wizards) | **Interactive Server, per-page** (`-int Server`) | Only the components that need interactivity opt in with `@rendermode`. The rest stays static. Server-side execution, full .NET access, no API layer needed. |
| Most pages need rich interactivity (dashboards, drag-and-drop, chat) | **Interactive Server, global** (`-int Server -ai`) | Every component is interactive by default. Consistent UX, simpler mental model. Trade-off: every user holds a SignalR circuit on the server. |
| Network latency is a problem, users are on mobile/poor connections, or the app must work offline | **Interactive WebAssembly** (`-int WebAssembly`) | Code runs in the browser. Eliminates round-trip latency but requires a `.Client` project, API layer for data access, and downloads the .NET runtime to the browser on first visit. For offline support, enable PWA: add a service worker and manifest after scaffolding (not included in the template by default). |
| Fast initial load (Server) + low latency after (WebAssembly) | **Interactive Auto** (`-int Auto`) | First visit uses Server; subsequent visits use cached WebAssembly runtime. Most complex setup — see Auto constraints below. Only choose when both Server and WebAssembly constraints apply. |

**Default recommendation:** Start with `-int Server` (per-page). It covers the vast majority of apps. Upgrade to global or WebAssembly only when a specific requirement demands it.

### Auto Mode Constraints

Auto mode means your component code runs on the server first, then in the browser on subsequent visits. This creates real constraints:

- **All interactive components must live in the `.Client` project** — same as WebAssembly.
- **No direct server access** from interactive components — no EF `DbContext`, no file system, no server-only services. All data access must go through HTTP APIs.
- **Both `Program.cs` files must register matching services** — the server and client DI containers must both provide implementations for any service an interactive component injects.
- **Code must not assume its execution environment** — no `HttpContext` access, no browser-only APIs without `RendererInfo` guards.
- **Test in both modes** — a component that works on Server during development may break on WebAssembly in production (second visit). Test both paths.

### Don'ts

- Don't pick WebAssembly "because it's cool" — it adds a `.Client` project, forces API-mediated data access, and downloads ~10MB to the browser on first visit.
- Don't pick Auto unless you can articulate why Server alone and WebAssembly alone are both insufficient.
- Don't pick global interactivity for apps where most pages are read-only content — per-page keeps the static pages fast and reduces server memory.

## Scaffold the Project

### Static SSR Only (display data + simple forms)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int None
```

No interactive runtime. Enhanced navigation enabled by default via `blazor.web.js`.

### Interactive Server, Per-Page (recommended default)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server
```

Pages are static by default. Add `@rendermode InteractiveServer` to components that need interactivity.

### Interactive Server, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -ai
```

All pages interactive via `<Routes @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />` in `App.razor`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly
```

Creates `{AppName}` (server) and `{AppName}.Client` (WebAssembly) projects. Interactive components must live in `.Client`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly -ai
```

### Interactive Auto, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto
```

### Interactive Auto, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto -ai
```

### With Authentication

Append `-au Individual` to any command above:

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -au Individual
```

# Create a Blazor Web App

## Before You Start — Gather Requirements

If the user's request doesn't make the following clear, ask before scaffolding:

1. **What does the app do?** List the main screens/features (e.g., "product catalog with search and shopping cart").
2. **What kind of interactivity is needed?** Displaying data and forms? Real-time updates? Offline support? Rich drag-and-drop UI?
3. **Deployment environment?** Internet-facing? Intranet? Mobile users on slow connections?
4. **Authentication needed?** Anonymous? Individual accounts? Organizational (Azure AD)?

## Pick the Right Interactivity Level

Blazor render modes are a progression scale. Start at the simplest level that satisfies the requirements and only move up when there's a concrete reason.

```
Static SSR ──→ SSR + Enhanced Nav ──→ Interactive Server ──→ Interactive WebAssembly
 simplest                                                           most complex
```

### Decision Rules

| If the app needs... | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Display data, simple forms, links between pages | **Static SSR** (`-int None`) | No JS runtime, no circuit, no WebAssembly download. Forms work via HTML POST. Enhanced navigation makes it feel snappy. |
| Everything above + a few components with client-side behavior (live search, real-time updates, complex form wizards) | **Interactive Server, per-page** (`-int Server`) | Only the components that need interactivity opt in with `@rendermode`. The rest stays static. Server-side execution, full .NET access, no API layer needed. |
| Most pages need rich interactivity (dashboards, drag-and-drop, chat) | **Interactive Server, global** (`-int Server -ai`) | Every component is interactive by default. Consistent UX, simpler mental model. Trade-off: every user holds a SignalR circuit on the server. |
| Network latency is a problem, users are on mobile/poor connections, or the app must work offline | **Interactive WebAssembly** (`-int WebAssembly`) | Code runs in the browser. Eliminates round-trip latency but requires a `.Client` project, API layer for data access, and downloads the .NET runtime to the browser on first visit. For offline support, enable PWA: add a service worker and manifest after scaffolding (not included in the template by default). |
| Fast initial load (Server) + low latency after (WebAssembly) | **Interactive Auto** (`-int Auto`) | First visit uses Server; subsequent visits use cached WebAssembly runtime. Most complex setup — see Auto constraints below. Only choose when both Server and WebAssembly constraints apply. |

**Default recommendation:** Start with `-int Server` (per-page). It covers the vast majority of apps. Upgrade to global or WebAssembly only when a specific requirement demands it.

### Auto Mode Constraints

Auto mode means your component code runs on the server first, then in the browser on subsequent visits. This creates real constraints:

- **All interactive components must live in the `.Client` project** — same as WebAssembly.
- **No direct server access** from interactive components — no EF `DbContext`, no file system, no server-only services. All data access must go through HTTP APIs.
- **Both `Program.cs` files must register matching services** — the server and client DI containers must both provide implementations for any service an interactive component injects.
- **Code must not assume its execution environment** — no `HttpContext` access, no browser-only APIs without `RendererInfo` guards.
- **Test in both modes** — a component that works on Server during development may break on WebAssembly in production (second visit). Test both paths.

### Don'ts

- Don't pick WebAssembly "because it's cool" — it adds a `.Client` project, forces API-mediated data access, and downloads ~10MB to the browser on first visit.
- Don't pick Auto unless you can articulate why Server alone and WebAssembly alone are both insufficient.
- Don't pick global interactivity for apps where most pages are read-only content — per-page keeps the static pages fast and reduces server memory.

## Scaffold the Project

### Static SSR Only (display data + simple forms)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int None
```

No interactive runtime. Enhanced navigation enabled by default via `blazor.web.js`.

### Interactive Server, Per-Page (recommended default)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server
```

Pages are static by default. Add `@rendermode InteractiveServer` to components that need interactivity.

### Interactive Server, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -ai
```

All pages interactive via `<Routes @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />` in `App.razor`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly
```

Creates `{AppName}` (server) and `{AppName}.Client` (WebAssembly) projects. Interactive components must live in `.Client`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly -ai
```

### Interactive Auto, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto
```

### Interactive Auto, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto -ai
```

### With Authentication

Append `-au Individual` to any command above:

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -au Individual
```

`-au Individual` scaffolds ASP.NET Core Identity with SQLite (CLI) or SQL Server (Visual Studio). Identity pages are always static SSR — they do not use interactive render modes.

The `blazor` template only supports `-au Individual`. For organizational auth (Microsoft Entra ID, Azure AD B2C), scaffold with `-au Individual` first, then replace the Identity provider with `Microsoft.Identity.Web` / OIDC middleware and configure the tenant in `appsettings.json`.

## What the Template Creates

### Single project (Static SSR, Server)

```
{AppName}/
├── Components/
│   ├── App.razor              # Root component — sets <HeadOutlet> and <Routes>
│   ├── Routes.razor           # Wraps <Router> with route discovery
│   ├── Layout/
│   │   ├── MainLayout.razor   # App shell with nav, header, footer

| WebAssembly, per-page (`-int WebAssembly`) | `assets/agents-md/webassembly-per-page.md` |
| WebAssembly, global (`-int WebAssembly -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/webassembly-global.md` |
| Auto, per-page (`-int Auto`) | `assets/agents-md/auto-per-page.md` |
| Auto, global (`-int Auto -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/auto-global.md` |

---
license: MIT
name: create-blazor-project
description: >
  Create a new ASP.NET Core web application or web site using Blazor.
  USE FOR: creating a new Blazor web app, scaffolding a new web project,
  starting a new web site, choosing render modes (Static SSR, Interactive Server,
  Interactive WebAssembly, Auto), running dotnet new blazor with the right options,
  setting up initial project structure.
  DO NOT USE FOR: adding features to existing projects, changing how an existing
  app renders, or component authoring (use author-component).
---

# Create a Blazor Web App

## Before You Start — Gather Requirements

If the user's request doesn't make the following clear, ask before scaffolding:

1. **What does the app do?** List the main screens/features (e.g., "product catalog with search and shopping cart").
2. **What kind of interactivity is needed?** Displaying data and forms? Real-time updates? Offline support? Rich drag-and-drop UI?
3. **Deployment environment?** Internet-facing? Intranet? Mobile users on slow connections?
4. **Authentication needed?** Anonymous? Individual accounts? Organizational (Azure AD)?

## Pick the Right Interactivity Level

Blazor render modes are a progression scale. Start at the simplest level that satisfies the requirements and only move up when there's a concrete reason.

```
Static SSR ──→ SSR + Enhanced Nav ──→ Interactive Server ──→ Interactive WebAssembly
 simplest                                                           most complex
```

### Decision Rules

| If the app needs... | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Display data, simple forms, links between pages | **Static SSR** (`-int None`) | No JS runtime, no circuit, no WebAssembly download. Forms work via HTML POST. Enhanced navigation makes it feel snappy. |
| Everything above + a few components with client-side behavior (live search, real-time updates, complex form wizards) | **Interactive Server, per-page** (`-int Server`) | Only the components that need interactivity opt in with `@rendermode`. The rest stays static. Server-side execution, full .NET access, no API layer needed. |
| Most pages need rich interactivity (dashboards, drag-and-drop, chat) | **Interactive Server, global** (`-int Server -ai`) | Every component is interactive by default. Consistent UX, simpler mental model. Trade-off: every user holds a SignalR circuit on the server. |
| Network latency is a problem, users are on mobile/poor connections, or the app must work offline | **Interactive WebAssembly** (`-int WebAssembly`) | Code runs in the browser. Eliminates round-trip latency but requires a `.Client` project, API layer for data access, and downloads the .NET runtime to the browser on first visit. For offline support, enable PWA: add a service worker and manifest after scaffolding (not included in the template by default). |
| Fast initial load (Server) + low latency after (WebAssembly) | **Interactive Auto** (`-int Auto`) | First visit uses Server; subsequent visits use cached WebAssembly runtime. Most complex setup — see Auto constraints below. Only choose when both Server and WebAssembly constraints apply. |

**Default recommendation:** Start with `-int Server` (per-page). It covers the vast majority of apps. Upgrade to global or WebAssembly only when a specific requirement demands it.

### Auto Mode Constraints

Auto mode means your component code runs on the server first, then in the browser on subsequent visits. This creates real constraints:

- **All interactive components must live in the `.Client` project** — same as WebAssembly.
- **No direct server access** from interactive components — no EF `DbContext`, no file system, no server-only services. All data access must go through HTTP APIs.
- **Both `Program.cs` files must register matching services** — the server and client DI containers must both provide implementations for any service an interactive component injects.
- **Code must not assume its execution environment** — no `HttpContext` access, no browser-only APIs without `RendererInfo` guards.
- **Test in both modes** — a component that works on Server during development may break on WebAssembly in production (second visit). Test both paths.

### Don'ts

- Don't pick WebAssembly "because it's cool" — it adds a `.Client` project, forces API-mediated data access, and downloads ~10MB to the browser on first visit.
- Don't pick Auto unless you can articulate why Server alone and WebAssembly alone are both insufficient.
- Don't pick global interactivity for apps where most pages are read-only content — per-page keeps the static pages fast and reduces server memory.

## Scaffold the Project

### Static SSR Only (display data + simple forms)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int None
```

No interactive runtime. Enhanced navigation enabled by default via `blazor.web.js`.

### Interactive Server, Per-Page (recommended default)

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server
```

Pages are static by default. Add `@rendermode InteractiveServer` to components that need interactivity.

### Interactive Server, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -ai
```

All pages interactive via `<Routes @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />` in `App.razor`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly
```

Creates `{AppName}` (server) and `{AppName}.Client` (WebAssembly) projects. Interactive components must live in `.Client`.

### Interactive WebAssembly, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int WebAssembly -ai
```

### Interactive Auto, Per-Page

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto
```

### Interactive Auto, Global

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Auto -ai
```

### With Authentication

Append `-au Individual` to any command above:

```shell
dotnet new blazor -o {AppName} -int Server -au Individual
```

`-au Individual` scaffolds ASP.NET Core Identity with SQLite (CLI) or SQL Server (Visual Studio). Identity pages are always static SSR — they do not use interactive render modes.

The `blazor` template only supports `-au Individual`. For organizational auth (Microsoft Entra ID, Azure AD B2C), scaffold with `-au Individual` first, then replace the Identity provider with `Microsoft.Identity.Web` / OIDC middleware and configure the tenant in `appsettings.json`.

## What the Template Creates

### Single project (Static SSR, Server)

```
{AppName}/
├── Components/
│   ├── App.razor              # Root component — sets <HeadOutlet> and <Routes>
│   ├── Routes.razor           # Wraps <Router> with route discovery
│   ├── Layout/
│   │   ├── MainLayout.razor   # App shell with nav, header, footer
│   │   └── MainLayout.razor.css
│   └── Pages/
│       └── Home.razor         # @page "/" — first page
├── Program.cs                 # Service registration and middleware
├── wwwroot/                   # Static files (CSS, images)
└── {AppName}.csproj
```

### Two projects (WebAssembly, Auto)

```
{AppName}/                     # Server project — hosts the app
├── Components/                # Server-only components (static SSR pages, layouts)
│   ├── App.razor
│   ├── Routes.razor
│   └── Layout/
├── Program.cs                 # Server Program.cs
└── {AppName}.Client/          # Client project — WebAssembly components
    ├── Pages/                 # Interactive components go HERE
    ├── Program.cs             # Client Program.cs
    └── _Imports.razor
```

**Rule:** Components using `InteractiveWebAssembly` or `InteractiveAuto` must live in the `.Client` project. They can reference shared code but cannot reference server-only types (EF `DbContext`, server-side services).

## Program.cs Wiring

The template generates the correct `Program.cs` for the chosen mode. Verify these registrations match your intent:

### Static SSR Only

```csharp
// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRazorComponents();

// ...

app.MapRazorComponents<App>();
```

### Server (per-page or global)

```csharp
builder.Services.AddRazorComponents()
    .AddInteractiveServerComponents();

// ...

app.MapRazorComponents<App>()
    .AddInteractiveServerRenderMode();
```

### WebAssembly (per-page or global)

```csharp
// Server Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRazorComponents()
    .AddInteractiveWebAssemblyComponents();

// ...

app.MapRazorComponents<App>()
    .AddInteractiveWebAssemblyRenderMode()
    .AddAdditionalAssemblies(typeof({AppName}.Client._Imports).Assembly);
```

```csharp
// Client Program.cs
builder.Services.AddAuthorizationCore();
// Register HttpClient, other client-side services
```

## Create Project AGENTS.md

After scaffolding, create an `AGENTS.md` file in the project root (next to the `.csproj`). For two-project setups, put it in the server project root.

Pick the matching template from `assets/agents-md/` based on the chosen mode:

| Mode | Template file |
|------|--------------|
| Static SSR (`-int None`) | `assets/agents-md/ssr-none.md` |
| Server, per-page (`-int Server`) | `assets/agents-md/server-per-page.md` |
| Server, global (`-int Server -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/server-global.md` |
| WebAssembly, per-page (`-int WebAssembly`) | `assets/agents-md/webassembly-per-page.md` |
| WebAssembly, global (`-int WebAssembly -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/webassembly-global.md` |
| Auto, per-page (`-int Auto`) | `assets/agents-md/auto-per-page.md` |
| Auto, global (`-int Auto -ai`) | `assets/agents-md/auto-global.md` |

Copy the template contents into the project's `AGENTS.md` and replace every `{AppName}` with the actual project name. If auth was scaffolded (`-au Individual`), add an `## Authentication` section noting that ASP.NET Core Identity is configured and that Identity pages under `Components/Account/` are always static SSR — do not add `@rendermode` to them.

**After scaffolding the project and creating AGENTS.md, continue implementing the features the user requested.** Remove default template pages (Counter, Weather) and replace them with the actual application pages.

### Auto (per-page or global)

```csharp
// Server Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRazorComponents()
    .AddInteractiveServerComponents()
    .AddInteractiveWebAssemblyComponents();

// ...

app.MapRazorComponents<App>()
    .AddInteractiveServerRenderMode()
    .AddInteractiveWebAssemblyRenderMode()
    .AddAdditionalAssemblies(typeof({AppName}.Client._Imports).Assembly);
```

## App.razor — Global vs Per-Page

The difference between global and per-page interactivity is entirely in `App.razor`:

### Per-page (default)

```razor
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <HeadOutlet />
</head>
<body>
    <Routes />
    <script src="_framework/blazor.web.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

No `@rendermode` on `<Routes>` or `<HeadOutlet>`. Individual pages opt in.

### Global

```razor
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <HeadOutlet @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />
</head>
<body>
    <Routes @rendermode="InteractiveServer" />
    <script src="_framework/blazor.web.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

Replace `InteractiveServer` with `InteractiveWebAssembly` or `InteractiveAuto` as appropriate.

## After Scaffolding

1. **Verify it builds:** `dotnet build`
2. **Run it:** `dotnet run` (in the server project if two-project setup)
3. **Add your first page:** Create a `.razor` file in `Components/Pages/` (server project) or `Pages/` (`.Client` project for WebAssembly components)

## Don'ts

- Don't use `dotnet new blazorwasm` — that creates a standalone WebAssembly SPA without server-side rendering. Use the `blazor` template with `-int WebAssembly` instead.
- Don't manually add `AddInteractiveServerComponents()` to a project created with `-int None` and expect it to work — you also need the `@rendermode` directives and potentially `App.razor` changes. Re-scaffold if the mode needs to change fundamentally.
- Don't put WebAssembly-targeted components in the server project — they'll work during prerender but fail after handoff.

Let me analyze the requirements:

- Offline support is critical (poor/no cellular connectivity)
- Low-latency UI (inspectors tap through items quickly)
- Mobile phones and tablets
- No authentication needed

This clearly points to **Interactive WebAssembly** mode:
- Offline support requires code running in the browser
- Low-latency UI means no server round-trips
- PWA with service worker for offline capability

I'll use `-int WebAssembly -ai` (global) since most pages need rich interactivity (checklists, photo capture, offline sync).

Let me read the webassembly-global agents-md template, then scaffold the project.

data.content: Now I'll implement the full inspection app. This is a complex task with offline/PWA support, IndexedDB storage, photo capture, and sync — let me build it out.

arguments.name: build-inspection-app
arguments.prompt:
You are building a Blazor WebAssembly app for construction site building inspectors. The project is already scaffolded at `/tmp/sv-57a71334e93045b9bda1e4f01a944158/SiteInspector/` with a solution containing:
- `SiteInspector/` (server project)
- `SiteInspector.Client/` (WebAssembly client project)

The app uses global Interactive WebAssembly mode. All interactive components go in the `.Client` project.

## Requirements
- Inspectors fill out safety checklists and add notes at each site visit
- Photos are attached to inspection reports
- Construction sites frequently have poor or no cellular connectivity — the app must continue working offline
- When connectivity returns, data syncs back to the server
- Inspectors use phones and tablets, not desktops
- Low-latency UI is critical — inspectors tap through checklist items quickly
- No authentication needed in the initial version

## What to implement

### 1. Remove template pages
Delete `Counter.razor` and `Weather.razor` from `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`.

### 2. Models (in `SiteInspector.Client/Models/`)
Create shared model classes:
- `Site.cs` - Id (string/guid), Name, Address
- `Inspection.cs` - Id, SiteId, InspectorName, StartedAt (DateTimeOffset), CompletedAt?, Notes, SyncStatus (enum: Pending, Synced, Failed)
- `ChecklistItem.cs` - Id, InspectionId, Category (string), Description, Status (enum: NotChecked, Pass, Fail, NA), Notes
- `Photo.cs` - Id, InspectionId, ChecklistItemId (optional), FileName, Base64Data, CapturedAt, Caption
- `SyncStatus.cs` enum

### 3. Local Storage Service with IndexedDB (offline-first)
Create a JavaScript interop file `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js` that manages an IndexedDB database called "SiteInspectorDb" with object stores: sites, inspections, checklistItems, photos.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/LocalStorageService.cs` that wraps the JS interop for CRUD operations on all entities.

### 4. Sync Service
Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/SyncService.cs`:
- Checks online status via JS interop
- When online, pushes pending inspections (with checklist items and photos) to server API
- Updates SyncStatus on items after sync
- Provides event for sync status changes

### 5. Server API Endpoints
In `SiteInspector/Program.cs`, add minimal API endpoints:
- `GET /api/sites` - returns list of sites
- `POST /api/sites` - create a site
- `POST /api/inspections/sync` - receives a batch of inspections with their checklist items and photos
- `GET /api/inspections/{siteId}` - get inspections for a site

For now, store server-side data in a simple in-memory list (no database needed for initial version). Create a `SiteInspector/Services/InMemoryDataStore.cs` singleton.

### 6. Pages (all in `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`)

**Home.razor** - Replace existing. Show list of sites with "Start Inspection" button for each. Button to add new site. Show sync status indicator.

**NewSite.razor** - Simple form: site name + address. Saves to local IndexedDB.

**Inspection.razor** (`/inspection/{SiteId}`) - Start a new inspection. Shows:
- Inspector name field (remembered from last use)
- Category-grouped checklist items with large tap targets (Pass/Fail/NA buttons)
- Notes field per item
- General notes for the inspection
- "Add Photo" button (per item or general)
- "Complete Inspection" button

Use a default safety checklist with categories:
- "Personal Protective Equipment": Hard hats, Safety vests, Steel-toe boots, Eye protection, Hearing protection
- "Fall Protection": Guardrails, Safety nets, Harness systems, Floor hole covers, Ladder condition
- "Electrical Safety": GFCI protection, Cord condition, Panel access, Lockout/tagout, Temporary wiring
- "Housekeeping": Walkways clear, Material storage, Waste disposal, Signage posted, Fire extinguishers

**PhotoCapture.razor** - Component (not a page) that uses `<InputFile>` to capture photos from camera or gallery. Converts to base64 and stores in IndexedDB. Shows thumbnail preview.

**InspectionHistory.razor** (`/history/{SiteId}`) - List past inspections for a site with their status.

### 7. Layout & Styling (mobile-first)
Update `SiteInspector.Client/Layout/MainLayout.razor` - Simple mobile app layout with a top bar and no sidebar nav. Keep it minimal for mobile.

Update/replace `SiteInspector.Client/Layout/NavMenu.razor` - Bottom navigation bar (mobile pattern) with icons for: Home, History, Sync.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/app.css` with mobile-first styles:
- Large touch targets (min 48px)
- Card-based layouts
- Status colors (green=pass, red=fail, gray=NA/unchecked)
- Bottom nav styling
- Responsive but primarily designed for phone screens

### 8. PWA / Offline Support
Add to `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/`:
- `manifest.json` - PWA manifest with app name "Site Inspector", construction-themed colors, display: standalone
- `service-worker.js` - Cache-first strategy for app shell assets, network-first for API calls (falls back to offline indicator)

In the server project's `App.razor`, add the manifest link and service worker registration script.

### 9. Online/Offline Status
Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/ConnectivityService.cs` - Uses JS interop to listen for online/offline events and expose current status.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/Components/SyncStatusIndicator.razor` - Shows a small badge/icon indicating online/offline state and pending sync count.

### 10. Program.cs Registration
Update `SiteInspector.Client/Program.cs` to register:
- HttpClient (pointing to server base address)
- LocalStorageService
- SyncService
- ConnectivityService

Update `SiteInspector/Program.cs` to register:
- InMemoryDataStore (singleton)
- CORS if needed
- The API endpoints

### 11. Create AGENTS.md
Create `/tmp/sv-57a71334e93045b9bda1e4f01a944158/SiteInspector/AGENTS.md` with project-specific guidance based on the WebAssembly global template, customized for this inspection app.

## Important Implementation Notes
- All UI interaction must be instant (no server round-trips for checklist taps) since data is stored locally in IndexedDB
- Use `IJSRuntime` for IndexedDB access
- The JS interop file should handle DB initialization, CRUD for all stores
- Large tap-friendly buttons for Pass/Fail/NA (think construction worker with gloves)
- Photos should be resized/compressed client-side before storing (max 1024px wide)
- The `_Imports.razor` should import the Models and Services namespaces
- Do NOT add `@rendermode` to pages — global interactivity is already set

## File organization summary
```
SiteInspector.Client/
├── Layout/
│   ├── MainLayout.razor
│   ├── MainLayout.razor.css  
│   └── NavMenu.razor
├── Components/
│   ├── PhotoCapture.razor
│   └── SyncStatusIndicator.razor
├── Models/
│   ├── Site.cs
│   ├── Inspection.cs
│   ├── ChecklistItem.cs
│   ├── Photo.cs
│   └── SyncStatus.cs
├── Services/
│   ├── LocalStorageService.cs
│   ├── SyncService.cs
│   └── ConnectivityService.cs
├── Pages/
│   ├── Home.razor
│   ├── NewSite.razor
│   ├── Inspection.razor
│   └── InspectionHistory.razor
├── wwwroot/
│   ├── js/
│   │   └── indexedDb.js
│   ├── manifest.json
│   └── service-worker.js
└── Program.cs

SiteInspector/
├── Services/
│   └── InMemoryDataStore.cs
├── Program.cs
└── Components/
    └── App.razor
```

Build the complete solution. After creating all files, run `dotnet build` on the solution to verify it compiles. Fix any errors.

Prompt to general-purpose agent (build-inspection-app-1):
You are building a Blazor WebAssembly app for construction site building inspectors. The project is already scaffolded at `/tmp/sv-57a71334e93045b9bda1e4f01a944158/SiteInspector/` with a solution containing:
- `SiteInspector/` (server project)
- `SiteInspector.Client/` (WebAssembly client project)

The app uses global Interactive WebAssembly mode. All interactive components go in the `.Client` project.

## Requirements
- Inspectors fill out safety checklists and add notes at each site visit
- Photos are attached to inspection reports
- Construction sites frequently have poor or no cellular connectivity — the app must continue working offline
- When connectivity returns, data syncs back to the server
- Inspectors use phones and tablets, not desktops
- Low-latency UI is critical — inspectors tap through checklist items quickly
- No authentication needed in the initial version

## What to implement

### 1. Remove template pages
Delete `Counter.razor` and `Weather.razor` from `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`.

### 2. Models (in `SiteInspector.Client/Models/`)
Create shared model classes:
- `Site.cs` - Id (string/guid), Name, Address
- `Inspection.cs` - Id, SiteId, InspectorName, StartedAt (DateTimeOffset), CompletedAt?, Notes, SyncStatus (enum: Pending, Synced, Failed)
- `ChecklistItem.cs` - Id, InspectionId, Category (string), Description, Status (enum: NotChecked, Pass, Fail, NA), Notes
- `Photo.cs` - Id, InspectionId, ChecklistItemId (optional), FileName, Base64Data, CapturedAt, Caption
- `SyncStatus.cs` enum

### 3. Local Storage Service with IndexedDB (offline-first)
Create a JavaScript interop file `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js` that manages an IndexedDB database called "SiteInspectorDb" with object stores: sites, inspections, checklistItems, photos.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/LocalStorageService.cs` that wraps the JS interop for CRUD operations on all entities.

### 4. Sync Service
Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/SyncService.cs`:
- Checks online status via JS interop
- When online, pushes pending inspections (with checklist items and photos) to server API
- Updates SyncStatus on items after sync
- Provides event for sync status changes

### 5. Server API Endpoints
In `SiteInspector/Program.cs`, add minimal API endpoints:
- `GET /api/sites` - returns list of sites
- `POST /api/sites` - create a site
- `POST /api/inspections/sync` - receives a batch of inspections with their checklist items and photos
- `GET /api/inspections/{siteId}` - get inspections for a site

For now, store server-side data in a simple in-memory list (no database needed for initial version). Create a `SiteInspector/Services/InMemoryDataStore.cs` singleton.

### 6. Pages (all in `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`)

**Home.razor** - Replace existing. Show list of sites with "Start Inspection" button for each. Button to add new site. Show sync status indicator.

**NewSite.razor** - Simple form: site name + address. Saves to local IndexedDB.

**Inspection.razor** (`/inspection/{SiteId}`) - Start a new inspection. Shows:
- Inspector name field (remembered from last use)
- Category-grouped checklist items with large tap targets (Pass/Fail/NA buttons)
- Notes field per item
- General notes for the inspection
- "Add Photo" button (per item or general)
- "Complete Inspection" button

Use a default safety checklist with categories:
- "Personal Protective Equipment": Hard hats, Safety vests, Steel-toe boots, Eye protection, Hearing protection
- "Fall Protection": Guardrails, Safety nets, Harness systems, Floor hole covers, Ladder condition
- "Electrical Safety": GFCI protection, Cord condition, Panel access, Lockout/tagout, Temporary wiring
- "Housekeeping": Walkways clear, Material storage, Waste disposal, Signage posted, Fire extinguishers

**PhotoCapture.razor** - Component (not a page) that uses `<InputFile>` to capture photos from camera or gallery. Converts to base64 and stores in IndexedDB. Shows thumbnail preview.

**InspectionHistory.razor** (`/history/{SiteId}`) - List past inspections for a site with their status.

### 7. Layout & Styling (mobile-first)
Update `SiteInspector.Client/Layout/MainLayout.razor` - Simple mobile app layout with a top bar and no sidebar nav. Keep it minimal for mobile.

Update/replace `SiteInspector.Client/Layout/NavMenu.razor` - Bottom navigation bar (mobile pattern) with icons for: Home, History, Sync.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/app.css` with mobile-first styles:
- Large touch targets (min 48px)
- Card-based layouts
- Status colors (green=pass, red=fail, gray=NA/unchecked)
- Bottom nav styling
- Responsive but primarily designed for phone screens

### 8. PWA / Offline Support
Add to `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/`:
- `manifest.json` - PWA manifest with app name "Site Inspector", construction-themed colors, display: standalone
- `service-worker.js` - Cache-first strategy for app shell assets, network-first for API calls (falls back to offline indicator)

In the server project's `App.razor`, add the manifest link and service worker registration script.

### 9. Online/Offline Status
Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/ConnectivityService.cs` - Uses JS interop to listen for online/offline events and expose current status.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/Components/SyncStatusIndicator.razor` - Shows a small badge/icon indicating online/offline state and pending sync count.

### 10. Program.cs Registration
Update `SiteInspector.Client/Program.cs` to register:
- HttpClient (pointing to server base address)
- LocalStorageService
- SyncService
- ConnectivityService

Update `SiteInspector/Program.cs` to register:
- InMemoryDataStore (singleton)
- CORS if needed
- The API endpoints

### 11. Create AGENTS.md
Create `/tmp/sv-57a71334e93045b9bda1e4f01a944158/SiteInspector/AGENTS.md` with project-specific guidance based on the WebAssembly global template, customized for this inspection app.

- All UI interaction must be instant (no server round-trips for checklist taps) since data is stored locally in IndexedDB
- Use `IJSRuntime` for IndexedDB access
- The JS interop file should handle DB initialization, CRUD for all stores
- Large tap-friendly buttons for Pass/Fail/NA (think construction worker with gloves)

arguments.file_text:
# Site Inspector - Agent Guidance

## Project Overview
Blazor WebAssembly app for construction site building inspectors. Offline-first architecture with IndexedDB for local storage and background sync to server.

## Architecture
- **Render Mode**: Global Interactive WebAssembly (set in App.razor). Do NOT add `@rendermode` to individual pages or components.
- **Server Project** (`SiteInspector/`): Hosts the Blazor app, serves static files, provides minimal API endpoints. Data stored in-memory (no database yet).
- **Client Project** (`SiteInspector.Client/`): All interactive UI lives here. Uses IndexedDB via JS interop for offline data persistence.
- **Offline-First**: All data operations go through `LocalStorageService` → IndexedDB. Sync to server is opportunistic via `SyncService`.

## Key Patterns
- JS Interop for IndexedDB access (`wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js`)
- Services registered as Scoped in WebAssembly (effectively singleton per tab)
- Mobile-first CSS with large touch targets (48px minimum)
- No authentication in current version
- Photos stored as base64 in IndexedDB, resized client-side to max 1024px

## Building & Running
```bash
cd SiteInspector
dotnet build SiteInspector.sln
dotnet run --project SiteInspector
```

## File Organization
- Models: `SiteInspector.Client/Models/`
- Services: `SiteInspector.Client/Services/`
- Pages: `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`
- Shared Components: `SiteInspector.Client/Components/`
- JS Interop: `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/js/`
- Server API: `SiteInspector/Program.cs` (minimal APIs)
- Server Services: `SiteInspector/Services/`

## Common Tasks
- Adding a new checklist category: Update `DefaultChecklist` dictionary in `Inspection.razor`
- Adding a new model: Create in `Models/`, add IndexedDB store in `indexedDb.js`, add CRUD methods in `LocalStorageService.cs`
- Adding API endpoints: Add to `SiteInspector/Program.cs` minimal API section

kind.prompt:
You are building a Blazor WebAssembly app for construction site building inspectors. The project is already scaffolded at `/tmp/sv-57a71334e93045b9bda1e4f01a944158/SiteInspector/` with a solution containing:
- `SiteInspector/` (server project)
- `SiteInspector.Client/` (WebAssembly client project)

The app uses global Interactive WebAssembly mode. All interactive components go in the `.Client` project.

## Requirements
- Inspectors fill out safety checklists and add notes at each site visit
- Photos are attached to inspection reports
- Construction sites frequently have poor or no cellular connectivity — the app must continue working offline
- When connectivity returns, data syncs back to the server
- Inspectors use phones and tablets, not desktops
- Low-latency UI is critical — inspectors tap through checklist items quickly
- No authentication needed in the initial version

## What to implement

### 1. Remove template pages
Delete `Counter.razor` and `Weather.razor` from `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`.

### 2. Models (in `SiteInspector.Client/Models/`)
Create shared model classes:
- `Site.cs` - Id (string/guid), Name, Address
- `Inspection.cs` - Id, SiteId, InspectorName, StartedAt (DateTimeOffset), CompletedAt?, Notes, SyncStatus (enum: Pending, Synced, Failed)
- `ChecklistItem.cs` - Id, InspectionId, Category (string), Description, Status (enum: NotChecked, Pass, Fail, NA), Notes
- `Photo.cs` - Id, InspectionId, ChecklistItemId (optional), FileName, Base64Data, CapturedAt, Caption
- `SyncStatus.cs` enum

### 3. Local Storage Service with IndexedDB (offline-first)
Create a JavaScript interop file `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/js/indexedDb.js` that manages an IndexedDB database called "SiteInspectorDb" with object stores: sites, inspections, checklistItems, photos.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/LocalStorageService.cs` that wraps the JS interop for CRUD operations on all entities.

### 4. Sync Service
Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/SyncService.cs`:
- Checks online status via JS interop
- When online, pushes pending inspections (with checklist items and photos) to server API
- Updates SyncStatus on items after sync
- Provides event for sync status changes

### 5. Server API Endpoints
In `SiteInspector/Program.cs`, add minimal API endpoints:
- `GET /api/sites` - returns list of sites
- `POST /api/sites` - create a site
- `POST /api/inspections/sync` - receives a batch of inspections with their checklist items and photos
- `GET /api/inspections/{siteId}` - get inspections for a site

For now, store server-side data in a simple in-memory list (no database needed for initial version). Create a `SiteInspector/Services/InMemoryDataStore.cs` singleton.

### 6. Pages (all in `SiteInspector.Client/Pages/`)

**Home.razor** - Replace existing. Show list of sites with "Start Inspection" button for each. Button to add new site. Show sync status indicator.

**NewSite.razor** - Simple form: site name + address. Saves to local IndexedDB.

**Inspection.razor** (`/inspection/{SiteId}`) - Start a new inspection. Shows:
- Inspector name field (remembered from last use)
- Category-grouped checklist items with large tap targets (Pass/Fail/NA buttons)
- Notes field per item
- General notes for the inspection
- "Add Photo" button (per item or general)
- "Complete Inspection" button

Use a default safety checklist with categories:
- "Personal Protective Equipment": Hard hats, Safety vests, Steel-toe boots, Eye protection, Hearing protection
- "Fall Protection": Guardrails, Safety nets, Harness systems, Floor hole covers, Ladder condition
- "Electrical Safety": GFCI protection, Cord condition, Panel access, Lockout/tagout, Temporary wiring
- "Housekeeping": Walkways clear, Material storage, Waste disposal, Signage posted, Fire extinguishers

**PhotoCapture.razor** - Component (not a page) that uses `<InputFile>` to capture photos from camera or gallery. Converts to base64 and stores in IndexedDB. Shows thumbnail preview.

**InspectionHistory.razor** (`/history/{SiteId}`) - List past inspections for a site with their status.

### 7. Layout & Styling (mobile-first)
Update `SiteInspector.Client/Layout/MainLayout.razor` - Simple mobile app layout with a top bar and no sidebar nav. Keep it minimal for mobile.

Update/replace `SiteInspector.Client/Layout/NavMenu.razor` - Bottom navigation bar (mobile pattern) with icons for: Home, History, Sync.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/app.css` with mobile-first styles:
- Large touch targets (min 48px)
- Card-based layouts
- Status colors (green=pass, red=fail, gray=NA/unchecked)
- Bottom nav styling
- Responsive but primarily designed for phone screens

### 8. PWA / Offline Support
Add to `SiteInspector.Client/wwwroot/`:
- `manifest.json` - PWA manifest with app name "Site Inspector", construction-themed colors, display: standalone
- `service-worker.js` - Cache-first strategy for app shell assets, network-first for API calls (falls back to offline indicator)

In the server project's `App.razor`, add the manifest link and service worker registration script.

### 9. Online/Offline Status
Create `SiteInspector.Client/Services/ConnectivityService.cs` - Uses JS interop to listen for online/offline events and expose current status.

Create `SiteInspector.Client/Components/SyncStatusIndicator.razor` - Shows a small badge/icon indicating online/offline state and pending sync count.

### 10. Program.cs Registration
Update `SiteInspector.Client/Program.cs` to register:
- HttpClient (pointing to server base address)
- LocalStorageService
- SyncService
- ConnectivityService

Update `SiteInspector/Program.cs` to register:
- InMemoryDataStore (singleton)
- CORS if needed
- The API endpoints

### 11. Create AGENTS.md
Create `/tmp/sv-57a71334e93045b9bda1e4f01a944158/SiteInspector/AGENTS.md` with project-specific guidance based on the WebAssembly global template, customized for this inspection app.

## Important Implementation Notes
- All UI interaction must be instant (no server round-trips for checklist taps) since data is stored locally in IndexedDB
- Use `IJSRuntime` for IndexedDB access
- The JS interop file should handle DB initialization, CRUD for all stores
- Large tap-friendly buttons for Pass/Fail/NA (think construction worker with gloves)
- Photos should be resized/compressed client-side before storing (max 1024px wide)
- The `_Imports.razor` should import the Models and Services namespaces
- Do NOT add `@rendermode` to pages — global interactivity is already set

## File organization summary
```
SiteInspector.Client/
├── Layout/
│   ├── MainLayout.razor
│   ├── MainLayout.razor.css  
│   └── NavMenu.razor
├── Components/
│   ├── PhotoCapture.razor
│   └── SyncStatusIndicator.razor
├── Models/
│   ├── Site.cs
│   ├── Inspection.cs
│   ├── ChecklistItem.cs
│   ├── Photo.cs
│   └── SyncStatus.cs
├── Services/
│   ├── LocalStorageService.cs
│   ├── SyncService.cs
│   └── ConnectivityService.cs
├── Pages/
│   ├── Home.razor
│   ├── NewSite.razor
│   ├── Inspection.razor
│   └── InspectionHistory.razor
├── wwwroot/
│   ├── js/
│   │   └── indexedDb.js
│   ├── manifest.json
│   └── service-worker.js
└── Program.cs

SiteInspector/
├── Services/
│   └── InMemoryDataStore.cs
├── Program.cs
└── Components/
    └── App.razor
```

Build the complete solution. After creating all files, run `dotnet build` on the solution to verify it compiles. Fix any errors.

data.content:
The app is built and compiles cleanly (0 errors, 0 warnings). Here's what was delivered:

## Project Structure

| Layer | Contents |
|-------|----------|
| **Models** | Site, Inspection, ChecklistItem, Photo, SyncStatus |
| **Services** | LocalStorageService (IndexedDB), ConnectivityService, SyncService |
| **Pages** | Home, NewSite, InspectionPage, InspectionHistory |
| **Components** | PhotoCapture (camera + resize), SyncStatusIndicator |
| **Layout** | Mobile bottom-nav, 48px+ tap targets, card UI |
| **PWA** | manifest.json, service-worker.js (cache-first shell) |
| **Server API** | 4 minimal endpoints + InMemoryDataStore |

## Key Architecture Decisions

- **IndexedDB** stores all data locally — checklist taps are instant with zero server dependency
- **PWA service worker** caches the app shell so it loads offline
- **Background sync** pushes completed inspections when connectivity returns
- **Photos** are resized to max 1024px client-side before storing as base64
- **Default safety checklist** with 20 items across 4 categories (PPE, Fall Protection, Electrical, Housekeeping)

Run with `cd SiteInspector/SiteInspector && dotnet run` — open on a phone/tablet and install as a PWA for the full offline experience.
